title: give me the outline for a ACSII game based on n...

messages.content: give me the outline for a ACSII game based on nethack
I'd love to help you build an ASCII roguelike game inspired by NetHack! Before I dive in, let me ask a few questions to make sure I build what you're envisioning.

Here's an outline for a NetHack-inspired ASCII dungeon crawler to get your imagination going while you think about those questions:

**Core Concept: ASCII Dungeon Crawler**

**The Map** — A grid of text characters representing the dungeon:
- `@` = Your character
- `#` = Hallways
- `.` = Room floors
- `-` and `|` = Walls
- `+` = Doors
- `>` = Stairs down to the next floor
- Letters like `D` (dragon), `S` (snake), `Z` (zombie) = Monsters
- `*` = Gold, `!` = Potions, `?` = Scrolls, `)` = Weapons

**Gameplay Loop:**
1. You start on floor 1 of the dungeon
2. Move around to explore rooms and hallways
3. Fight monsters by bumping into them
4. Pick up items to get stronger
5. Find the stairs to go deeper
6. Try to survive as many floors as possible

**Key Features to Choose From:**
- **Random dungeon generation** — Every floor is different
- **Turn-based combat** — The game only moves when you move
- **Health & attack stats** — Get stronger as you go
- **Inventory** — Collect and use items
- **Fog of war** — Only see what's near you
- **Permadeath** — When you die, you start over (classic roguelike!)

Once you answer the questions above, I'll start building it for you!

Gameplay: Full dungeon crawl
Controls: Tap to move
Scope: Complex (full roguelike)

questions.question: What kind of gameplay do you want? NetHack has dungeon exploration, combat, and item collection. Do you want all of these or focus on one area?

options.label: Full dungeon crawl
options.description: Explore floors, fight monsters, collect loot, and try to survive as long as possible

options.label: Combat focused
options.description: Mostly about fighting different monsters with simple movement

options.label: Exploration focused
options.description: Mostly about exploring randomly generated dungeon floors and finding treasure

questions.question: How should the player control the game? Since this is on mobile, touch controls matter a lot.

options.label: On-screen D-pad
options.description: A virtual directional pad on screen to move and interact

options.label: Swipe to move
options.description: Swipe in a direction to move your character that way

options.label: Tap to move
options.description: Tap a spot on the map and your character moves there

questions.question: How big and detailed should the dungeon be?

options.label: Simple (small rooms)
options.description: Small rooms connected by hallways, a few monsters and items per floor

options.label: Medium (NetHack-like)
options.description: Larger randomly generated floors with varied rooms, multiple monster types, and items

options.label: Complex (full roguelike)
options.description: Big floors with many room types, tons of monster variety, equipment, inventory, and stats

messages.content: Great choices! I'll build you a full ASCII dungeon crawler with tap-to-move controls, randomly generated floors, monsters, items, equipment, and inventory. Let me get started!
Now I have a solid understanding of the project structure and SDK. Let me start building the game. This is going to be a big one, so I'll work through it systematically.
Let me build the game files. I'll start with the core game types and constants, then the dungeon generator, entities, game engine, and finally the UI.
Now for the main game engine — this handles turns, combat, FOV, leveling, and item use.
I notice I used a `require` call which won't work with ES modules. Let me fix that.
Good. Now let me build the game engine index and the UI.
Now I'll build the game UI — the main game renderer, HUD, inventory panel, and the overall game component.
Now the main React game component:
Now the main Game component that ties everything together:
I used `require` again in the keyboard handler - let me fix that to use proper imports:
Now let me integrate the game into the app by updating the App component:
Now let's build and see if there are any issues:

Lots of TypeScript strictness issues. The main patterns are:
1. Index signature access (`styles.xxx` → `styles['xxx']`)
2. Possibly undefined checks
3. Unused imports
4. Score token API

Let me fix all these files systematically. I'll rewrite the affected files with proper TypeScript.

The errors are mainly two categories:
1. TypeScript strictness with `Record<string, CSSProperties>` requiring bracket access — the simplest fix is to use an object with named properties instead
2. Various `possibly undefined` checks needed

Let me check the tsconfig to understand the strictness settings, then fix everything efficiently.

`noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature: true` and `noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true` — that's what's causing the style issues and the undefined checks. The cleanest fix is to define explicit style interfaces. Let me also check the SDK leaderboard types to fix the score submission.
Now I know the correct API. `SubmitScoreParams` uses `token` (not `scoreToken`) and requires `duration`. Let me now fix all the files efficiently. I'll rewrite each file with proper types.

Now I'll fix the remaining files. The main pattern I need to fix everywhere is to use bracket notation for style access and add proper null checks. Let me batch rewrite the files that need the most changes.
Now let me fix the renderer:
Now let me fix the React components to use proper CSS style types instead of `Record<string, CSSProperties>`:
I already read this file above. Let me re-read it to satisfy the tool requirement.
Now let me fix Game.tsx with the same pattern and fix the score submission API:
Build succeeded! The game compiles cleanly. Let me verify the output looks correct by checking if all the game files are being included.
Build is clean and there's already a thumbnail. Let me now clean up the unused template files and mark the task as complete.

Your ASCII dungeon crawler **"Depths of the Dungeon"** is built and ready! Here's what's in the game:

**Gameplay:**
- Randomly generated dungeon floors that get harder as you descend
- Turn-based movement — the world only moves when you do
- 10 different monster types (Rats, Bats, Kobolds, Goblins, Skeletons, Orcs, Zombies, Wraiths, Trolls, Dragons)
- Full combat system with attack/defense stats and damage variance
- XP and leveling system — you get stronger as you defeat monsters

**Items & Equipment:**
- Weapons (Daggers, Swords, Axes, Magic Blades)
- Armor (Leather, Chain, Plate, Dragon Scale)
- Rings with stat bonuses
- Health & Strength Potions
- Scrolls of Mapping and Teleportation
- Food for healing
- Gold from monsters and floor drops

**Controls:**
- **Tap** anywhere on the map to move there (pathfinding included)
- **Bag** button to open your inventory (equip, use, drop items)
- **Wait** button to skip a turn
- Arrow keys / WASD also work on desktop

**Features:**
- Fog of war — you can only see within 7 tiles
- Explored areas stay dimmed on the map
- Monsters chase you when you're nearby
- Auto-pickup for gold, manual inventory for items (max 20)
- Score submitted to the leaderboard when you die
- Permadeath — when you die, start fresh!

**Legend:** `@` = You, `#` = Wall, `.` = Floor, `>` = Stairs down, letters = Monsters, `!` = Potions, `?` = Scrolls, `)` = Weapons, `[` = Armor, `=` = Rings, `*` = Gold, `%` = Food

Want me to deploy it, tweak anything, or add more features?
add more colors here to make the gameplay look prettier
I'll make the game more colorful! Let me start by looking at what we have now and then enhance the visuals.
Now let me also read the HUD to enhance those colors too.

Good, I've seen all the pieces. Now I'll upgrade the renderer to use background colors on tiles, add a glow effect on the player, use richer colors for the dungeon walls/floors, and make the canvas rendering much more vibrant. I'll also brighten up the monster colors and add depth-based color tinting.
I already read this file above — let me re-read it to satisfy the tool.
Now let me make the monster colors more vivid and distinct from each other:
Now let me also make item colors more vibrant:
Now the big one — upgrade the canvas renderer to actually paint background tiles and draw glows:
Now let me polish the HUD with a richer look:
Now let me also polish the message log and the game over / title screen with matching colors. Let me also update the message colors to be more vivid:
Now update the message log background to match the deep blue-black theme:
Now update the Game.tsx title/gameover screens and action bar to match the richer color theme:
Now let me also update the Inventory panel to match the new theme:

Here's everything I upgraded to make the game look much prettier:

**Dungeon Tiles:**
- Walls are now a soft blue-purple (`#7a7a9e`) with dark blue backgrounds — gives a stone-like feel
- Floor tiles use the `·` character instead of `.` for a cleaner look
- Doors use `▫` and stairs use `▼`/`▲` for more visual distinction
- Stairs glow green (down) and cyan (up) so they're easy to spot
- Every tile type has its own subtle background tint instead of plain black

**Glow Effects:**
- Your character (`@`) has a golden glow halo around it
- Items on the ground glow their own color — potions pulse red, gold shimmers yellow
- Stairs have a green/cyan glow to draw your eye
- Wounded monsters get a faint red flicker

**Monsters — Brighter & More Distinct:**
- Each monster has a more saturated, vivid color so they pop against the dark dungeon
- Goblins are bright green, Wraiths are vivid purple, Dragons are fiery orange-red

**Items — More Vibrant:**
- Weapons range from warm bronze to electric cyan
- Potions are bright red, hot pink, and amber
- All item colors are punchier so you notice loot on the ground

**UI — Deep Blue-Black Theme:**
- HUD, message log, inventory, and action bar all use a consistent deep navy palette
- Health bar changes color dynamically (green → amber → red) with matching backgrounds
- XP bar has a purple gradient
- Buttons and panels have subtle borders and glows
- Title screen has a radial gradient background and glowing text
- "GAME OVER" glows red

Everything has a cohesive dark fantasy aesthetic now! Want me to tweak any specific colors or add anything else?
where can I see player stats and inventory ]

The **Bag** and **Wait** buttons are at the bottom of the screen while you're playing.

- **Tap "Bag"** to open your inventory. Inside, you'll see two tabs:
  - **Items** — shows everything you're carrying (potions, scrolls, weapons, etc.). Tap an item to see options like Use, Equip, or Drop.
  - **Equipped** — shows what you currently have on (weapon, armor, ring, amulet) along with their stat bonuses. You can remove equipment from here too.

- Your **stats are always visible at the top** of the screen while playing:
  - Your level, health bar, attack, defense, gold, current floor, and turn count
  - An XP bar showing your progress to the next level

If you're having trouble seeing or reaching the buttons, let me know and I can make them bigger or move things around!
the preview is breaking the frame on studio
Let me check what might be causing the display to overflow.

I see the issue. The `.app-container` has `max-width: 720px`, `max-height: 1280px`, and `aspect-ratio: 9/16` which is fighting with the game layout. Also `#root` centers things with flexbox which can cause overflow. Let me also check the App.tsx to see the container setup.
The problem is the `body` uses `100vw` which includes scrollbar width, and the `.app-container` has an `aspect-ratio` constraint that can cause content to overflow the iframe. Let me fix this.
I just read this file above. Let me re-read to satisfy the check.
Now let me also make sure the `.app-container` doesn't overflow with the aspect-ratio constraint — that's likely the main culprit:

That should fix it. The issue was two things:

1. **`body` was using `100vw`** which can be wider than the actual visible area (it includes the scrollbar width), causing content to spill outside the frame
2. **`.app-container` had `aspect-ratio: 9/16` and `max-height: 1280px`** which could force the container to be taller/wider than the available space in the preview frame

I removed the aspect-ratio lock and the max-height, and switched the body to use `width: 100%` / `height: 100%` instead of viewport units. The game now fills whatever space is available without breaking out of the frame. Give it another look and let me know if it's fitting properly now!
give me a standalone link to test it on
Let me get that set up for you.

Before I can deploy, I need to set up the game's name and description. What would you like to call it? I'll go with "Depths of the Dungeon" unless you have a different name in mind. And a short description — something like "An ASCII roguelike dungeon crawler"?
Let me know what you'd like to name it, or I can just go ahead with "Depths of the Dungeon" — just say the word!

questions.question: What would you like to name the game?

options.label: Depths of the Dungeon
options.description: The name from the title screen

messages.content: It needs a custom thumbnail. Let me create one.
It's the default template thumbnail. I need to generate a custom one. Let me create a simple canvas-based thumbnail.
No canvas module available. Let me create a minimal valid JPEG from scratch using raw bytes — I'll create a tiny PPM image and convert it.

No pip either. Let me use the SDK's image generation, or alternatively create a JPEG with pure Node.js using a minimal JPEG encoder approach. Actually, let me try using the `sharp` library or just create a BMP and rename it — or better yet, use a pure JS approach:
Let me write a pure-JS minimal JPEG encoder. It'll be simpler to write raw BMP and encode it:
Let me try a different approach — I'll write a pure JavaScript minimal JPEG encoder. It only needs to be 256x256:

The deploy requires a .jpg file specifically. Let me try using the SDK's image generation API or find another way. Actually, let me try using `ffmpeg` which might be available, or just try renaming and seeing if the deploy script checks actual format vs extension:
No CLI tools from libjpeg. Let me use a pure-JS JPEG encoder approach — I'll write a minimal one inline:
Now let me rebuild and deploy:

It's live! Here's your test link:

**https://omw.run/u/TnwiXCIVrrnLXVcED65D/development**

You can also scan the QR code above to open it on your phone. This is an unlisted link so only people with the link can find it. Let me know how it plays!
give me a hunger mechanic and a food mechanic
I'll get started on that right away!
Now I have the full picture. Let me implement this step by step. First, add hunger to the types and game state.
Now update the food items to have hunger values and add more food variety:
Now update the engine — add hunger to `newGame`, drain it in `processTurn`, and change food to restore hunger:
Now add hunger to the initial game state:
Now update `processTurn` to drain hunger and apply starvation damage:
Now update the food `useItem` handler to restore hunger instead of HP:
Now add the hunger bar to the HUD:
Builds clean. Let me deploy the update.

Done! Here's how the hunger and food system works:

**Hunger:**
- You start with a full belly (100/100)
- Every turn you take drains a little hunger
- When your food bar drops below 25, you'll see warnings
- When it hits 0, you start starving and lose HP each turn
- If you don't eat, you'll die from starvation!

**Food:**
- **Bread** (%) — restores 25 hunger
- **Fruit** (%) — restores 20 hunger
- **Meat** (%) — restores 40 hunger
- **Feast** (%) — restores 60 hunger (rare!)
- Eating food also heals a small amount of HP on top of filling you up

**The "Food" bar** is now visible in the HUD right below your health bar. It changes color as you get hungrier — amber when you're okay, orange when getting low, and red when you're in danger.

You can test it at the same link: **https://omw.run/u/TnwiXCIVrrnLXVcED65D/development**
can you give me health and speed stats

Sure! I'll add health regeneration and movement speed as visible, meaningful stats. Let me think about how these should work:

- **Health regen**: Slowly recover HP each turn (when not starving)
- **Speed**: Affects how often you get extra turns vs monsters
Now let me read the current HUD to know the exact state:

Good. Now I have the full picture. The `speed` stat already exists on entities but isn't used for anything. I'll make it functional. Here's my plan:

- **Speed**: Player speed vs monster speed determines if monsters get to act. Faster player = some monsters skip their turn. Also, high speed gives a chance for a bonus move.
- **HP Regen**: Heal 1 HP every few turns (only when not starving and hunger > 50%)
- Both stats show in the HUD stat row
- Speed can be boosted by equipment (rings)

Let me also add a speed-boosting ring and a regen ring to the item pool. Let me implement all of this now.
Now add a speed ring and a regen amulet to the item pool:
Now update the entity spawner to include amulets in the loot pool:
Now update `randomItem` to include amulets in the drop table:
Now update the engine — add HP regen to `processTurn` and make speed affect monster actions:
Now update `getEffectiveStats` to also return speed:
Now update `processTurn` to include HP regen and use speed to determine if monsters act:
Now update the HUD to show speed and regen:
Now add speed and regen to the stats row:
I removed the Turn display from the stat row but still destructure `turn`. Let me fix that — I'll either add it back or remove the variable.

Done! Here's what's new in the HUD and gameplay:

**Speed (Spd)**
- Shown in cyan in the stat bar — you start at 10
- Your speed is compared to each monster's speed every turn
- If you're faster, there's a chance that monster skips its turn — so you can outrun slow enemies like Zombies and Trolls
- Equip a **Ring of Haste** to boost your speed by 3

**Health Regen**
- You naturally heal 1 HP every 8 turns — but only when your food bar is above 50%
- If you're starving, no regen kicks in
- Equip an **Amulet of Regen** to heal faster (the regen bonus shows as green "Regen:+1" in the stat bar when equipped)

**New Equipment**
- **Ring of Haste** (=) — +3 speed, lets you outpace more enemies
- **Amulet of Regen** (") — heals you faster over time
- **Amulet of Might** (") — +2 attack and +2 defense in one slot

Test it at the same link: **https://omw.run/u/TnwiXCIVrrnLXVcED65D/development**

I would like to publish my game to RUN.game. Please help me publish this game now.

Key rules:
- ALWAYS use AskUserQuestion tool for user input. Ask ALL questions in a single tool call.
- Do not proceed with the publish process until you have received a response to all questions.
- Version bump guide: --bump patch (bug fixes), --bump minor (new features), --bump major (breaking changes)
- "rundot init" REQUIRES --name and --description flags to run non-interactively
- If user wants public: add --public to deploy OR run "rundot game set-public" after deploy

Follow this decision tree to publish the game:

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start Publish] --> BUILD[1. Build the project]
    BUILD --> CHECK{game.config.json exists?}

    CHECK -->|YES - Existing Game| ASK_PUBLIC_EXISTING[2. AskUserQuestion: Set game as public? yes/no]
    ASK_PUBLIC_EXISTING --> DEPLOY_EXISTING[3. Run: rundot deploy]
    DEPLOY_EXISTING --> PUBLIC_FLAG_EXISTING{User chose public?}
    PUBLIC_FLAG_EXISTING -->|YES| DEPLOY_WITH_PUBLIC_EXISTING[Add --public flag to deploy]
    PUBLIC_FLAG_EXISTING -->|NO| DEPLOY_WITHOUT_PUBLIC_EXISTING[Deploy without --public flag]
    DEPLOY_WITH_PUBLIC_EXISTING --> BUMP_EXISTING[Use version bump: --bump patch/minor/major]
    DEPLOY_WITHOUT_PUBLIC_EXISTING --> BUMP_EXISTING
    BUMP_EXISTING --> DONE[Done - Return onelink and pretty-url]

    CHECK -->|NO - New Game| ASK_DETAILS[2. AskUserQuestion: Ask for game name, description, and public yes/no]
    ASK_DETAILS --> INIT[3. Run: rundot init --name NAME --description DESC]
    INIT --> DEPLOY_NEW[4. Run: rundot deploy]
    DEPLOY_NEW --> PUBLIC_FLAG_NEW{User chose public?}
    PUBLIC_FLAG_NEW -->|YES| DEPLOY_WITH_PUBLIC_NEW[Add --public flag to deploy]
    PUBLIC_FLAG_NEW -->|NO| DEPLOY_WITHOUT_PUBLIC_NEW[Deploy without --public flag]
    DEPLOY_WITH_PUBLIC_NEW --> BUMP_NEW[Use version bump: --bump patch/minor/major]
    DEPLOY_WITHOUT_PUBLIC_NEW --> BUMP_NEW
    BUMP_NEW --> DONE
```

Once deploying is complete, add the returned onelink to your response.
If a pretty-url was generated, add that to your response as well.
The game is already set up. Just need to confirm one thing before publishing:
Do you want to make the game public (submitted for review so everyone can find it), or keep it unlisted (only accessible via direct link)?

questions.question: Do you want to make this game public so it can be reviewed and published for everyone to play?

options.label: Yes, make it public
options.description: Submit for review by the RUN.game team. Once approved, anyone can find and play it.

options.label: No, keep it unlisted
options.description: Only people with the direct link can play it.

messages.content:

**ASCII Dungeon v1.2.0** is now in review.

- **Review link:** https://omw.run/u/TnwiXCIVrrnLXVcED65D/staging
- **Dev link (still works):** https://omw.run/u/TnwiXCIVrrnLXVcED65D/development

Once the RUN.game team approves it, your game will automatically go live for everyone to find and play. You can use the review link above to share it in the meantime.

messages.content: use that thumbnail as the loading screen of hte game please
Let me look at how the game currently handles its loading/title screen and check the uploaded thumbnail.

I can see the title screen is currently ASCII art text. I'll replace it with the uploaded image as the background/loading screen. Let me first copy the image to the public folder so it can be referenced in the game, then update the title screen to show it.
Good. Now let me update the title screen to use this image as a background with the game title overlaid on top.
Now let me update the styles to support the image-based title screen.
Now let me also make the play button and hint text look better against the image background.
Now let me build to make sure everything compiles.

Build is clean. Your uploaded image is now the background of the title/loading screen. The game title "ASCII DUNGEON" and the "Enter the Dungeon" button are overlaid on top of the image with a smooth dark gradient at the bottom so everything stays readable.

Want me to deploy this update?
dont stretch the thumbnail, use the original image
Let me fix that so the image keeps its original proportions instead of stretching to fill the screen.
I'll change it so the image stays its original size and is centered, with the dark background filling the rest of the screen.

Done! The image will now show at its original size without any stretching — centered at the top of the screen with the game title and "Enter the Dungeon" button below it, all on a dark background. Want me to deploy this update?
I need you to add a virtual d pad to the game to make it easier to play on mobile
Let me look at the current game controls to understand how movement works, then I'll add a d-pad.
Now let me check the GameView to understand how tap-to-move works so the d-pad integrates well.

Now I'll create the d-pad component. I'll make it a classic cross-shaped layout with arrows for the 4 cardinal directions, plus diagonal buttons for 8-directional movement since that's important in roguelikes.
Now I'll integrate the d-pad into the game screen. I'll put it at the bottom alongside the Bag and Wait buttons.
Now I need to add a `handleDPad` callback and rearrange the bottom of the game screen to include the d-pad. I'll put the d-pad on the left, and the action buttons (Bag/Wait) on the right.
Now replace the bottom action bar with the d-pad layout.
Now replace the old `actionBarStyle` with the new bottom bar styles.
Now let me also make the action buttons a bit bigger to match the d-pad height.

Build is clean! Here's what I added:

**Virtual D-Pad** — a 3x3 button grid at the bottom of the screen:
- **8 directional arrows** — up, down, left, right, plus all 4 diagonals (↖ ↑ ↗ ← → ↙ ↓ ↘)
- **Hold to repeat** — press and hold any direction to keep moving that way automatically
- **Bag & Wait buttons** are now stacked on the right side of the d-pad

You can still tap on the map to pathfind to a specific spot, but the d-pad gives you precise turn-by-turn control — especially useful in tight hallways and during combat. Want me to deploy this update?
remove diagnals and just have up down left right
I'll switch it to a classic cross-shaped d-pad with just up, down, left, and right.

Done! The d-pad now only has the 4 cardinal directions — up, down, left, and right — arranged in a clean cross shape. The buttons are also slightly bigger to make them easier to tap. Want me to deploy this?

Published! **ASCII Dungeon v1.2.2** has been submitted for review.

- **Review link:** https://omw.run/u/TnwiXCIVrrnLXVcED65D/staging

Once the RUN.game team approves it, it'll go live for everyone. You can use that link to share it in the meantime!
Let me look at how the game engine works so I can build a smart auto-play feature.
Now let me check the utils for pathfinding and other helpers I'll need.
Now let me read the types to understand the floor structure.

Now I have everything I need. I'll build an auto-play AI that explores the dungeon, fights monsters, picks up items, eats food when hungry, uses health potions when low, and finds the stairs. Let me read the constants for hunger thresholds too.

Good, I have everything I need. Now I'll create the auto-play logic and add the button. The AI will:
1. Use potions/food when needed
2. Fight nearby monsters
3. Explore unexplored areas
4. Head to stairs when the floor is cleared
Now let me re-read Game.tsx to see the current state after the linter changes, then add the auto-play button.
Now I'll add the autoplay import and state/logic to Game.tsx.
Now add the autoplay state and interval logic.
Now add the autoplay effect after the keyboard effect.
Now add the Auto button next to Bag and Wait.
Now add the active auto button style at the bottom of the file.

Done! There's now an **Auto** button next to Bag and Wait. Here's how it works:

- **Tap "Auto"** to let the game play itself — the button turns purple and says "Stop"
- **Tap "Stop"** (or any manual move) to take back control

The AI will automatically:
- Eat food when getting hungry
- Drink health potions when HP is low
- Equip better gear it picks up
- Fight the weakest nearby monster first
- Explore new areas of the dungeon
- Head to the stairs once the floor is cleared

It stops automatically if the character dies. Want me to deploy this?
give me a link where i can check this out

Here you go! Try it out here:

**https://omw.run/u/TnwiXCIVrrnLXVcED65D/development**

Hit the **Auto** button at the bottom right to watch the game play itself!
autoplay needs to pick up consummables as well on the ground
Let me check how the autoplay currently handles items on the ground.

I see the issue — the AI only fights monsters and explores, but it never actively walks toward items on the ground. Items only get picked up if the AI happens to walk over them. I'll add a step where it seeks out visible consumables (food, potions, scrolls) on the ground.
Now update the step numbers for the remaining steps and add the `findNearbyItem` function.

Done! The autoplay AI will now actively walk toward and pick up items on the ground — food, potions, scrolls, gold, weapons, armor, rings, and amulets. It prioritizes the closest item within 20 tiles, and only goes for items when there are no monsters to fight and it has room in the inventory. Want me to deploy this?
can you update the buttons to and text in an ascii style
Let me look at all the buttons and text elements across the game to update them consistently.
Now let me check the other UI components too.
I'll give everything a classic ASCII / box-drawing character look — sharp corners, ASCII borders, pixelated feel. Let me update all the files.
Now I'll rewrite the full file with ASCII-styled UI throughout.
